1: The woe-oracle (hoy) targets the anonymous destroyer and betrayer -- likely Assyria, which has used other nations and will be discarded. The oracle operates on a law-of-retaliation principle: when you finish destroying, you will be destroyed; when you stop betraying, you will be betrayed. The MKT preserves the passive-future forms that make the reversal feel structural rather than merely retributive.
2: YHWH is flagged -- the direct address to the covenant God: LORD, be gracious to us; we wait for you. The arm of strength every morning is a daily-renewal image of divine help; not a crisis intervention but a covenantal sustenance renewed each day. The waiting (qivvinu) is active expectation, not passive resignation -- the same word for hope used in Isaiah 40:31 (those who wait on the LORD).
3: At the sound of the LORD's voice the peoples flee -- a theophanic motif where the divine voice itself causes panic and scattering. The distinction between the voice (which causes flight) and the rising (which causes scattering) is a two-stage theophany: first auditory, then visible. The nations' flight at divine speech anticipates the NT pattern of demons fleeing at Jesus' word.
4: The spoil is gathered like locusts stripping a field -- the simile runs both ways. The victor gathers the Assyrian's abandoned spoil as efficiently as a locust swarm strips crops: nothing is left. The locusts and grasshoppers evoke the plague imagery -- as Egypt was stripped by locusts (Exodus 10:14-15), Assyria will be stripped by a similar comprehensive plundering.
5: YHWH is flagged; tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) is flagged -- he has filled Zion with justice and righteousness. The MKT renders tsedaqah as righteousness alongside mishpat (justice). The filling of Zion with these qualities is an act of the exalted LORD dwelling on high -- divine attributes descend into the city from the transcendent LORD. The MKT's pairing preserves both the social-legal (mishpat) and the moral-relational (tsedaqah) dimensions.
6: YHWH is flagged -- the fear of the LORD is Zion's treasure. The stability of times is an unusual phrase -- emunat ittekha: the faithfulness/steadiness of your times. The MKT's stability of your times renders emunah as stability, capturing the firmness and reliability of the covenantal era. The list of treasures -- salvation, wisdom, knowledge, fear of the LORD -- is comprehensive; the fear of the LORD (the beginning of wisdom, Proverbs 9:10) is listed last as the crown of the sequence.
7: Shalom (peace, disp 2) is flagged -- the envoys of peace are weeping bitterly. The MKT renders shalom as peace -- the covenant-wholeness word. The envoys of peace represent whatever diplomatic and negotiating process Judah attempted with Assyria; they failed. The brave warriors (erelam) crying out and the peace-envoys weeping mark the total failure of both military and diplomatic options, driving the people to the prayer of v2.
8: Berit (covenant, disp 3) is flagged -- he has broken the covenant. The covenant broken is likely the treaty between Hezekiah and Sennacherib (2 Kings 18:14-17). The MKT's covenant carries the full OT legal-relational weight: this is not merely a violated political agreement but a sworn commitment that creates legal and moral obligations. The cities despised and men regarded as nothing -- the comprehensive destruction of civil society by the Assyrian advance.
9: The earth mourning (avlah) is a double entendre: the land fades/mourns as if in grief. Lebanon, Sharon, Bashan, and Carmel -- the four most fertile and productive regions of the land -- are listed as the regions now shaking off and wilting. These are not arbitrary place-names; they are the places of greatest natural abundance, now described as dried up (shaken off as in harvest). The MKT's shakes off for Carmel captures the image of a vine stripped bare.
10: YHWH is flagged -- the divine self-declaration of rising (equm) and being exalted (eromam) and lifted up (etnasse): three synonymous verbs of divine elevation corresponding to the Assyrian's arrogance of v8. The Assyrian despised everyone; now the LORD declares his counter-elevation. The triple rise-declaration is the structural inversion of the triple woe (v1).
11: Ruach is flagged (disp 4) -- your own breath, like fire, will consume you. The Assyrian's breath/spirit that has powered his pride will become the fire that destroys him. The MKT's breath captures the Hebrew ruach in its wind/breath sense here -- not the Spirit of God but the Assyrian's own vital force turned against him. Chaff and stubble are the Assyrian's works; fire is the Assyrian's own spirit.
12: The peoples burned like limestone -- the image is of thorough calcination: limestone burned until it becomes quicklime, destroyed to its chemical structure. Thornbushes set ablaze -- the briefest and most complete combustion. The two images together (rock reduced to powder, thornbush gone in a flash) represent comprehensive judgment that leaves nothing behind, working on both the enduring and the ephemeral.
13: YHWH is flagged (yada, know, disp 2) -- acknowledge my power. The call goes to both the far and the near: those who are distant witnesses and those who have seen the events close up. The MKT's acknowledge translates yada in its acknowledgment sense: not merely recognizing facts but giving appropriate recognition to divine power. The covenantal knowing-acknowledgment is the expected response to YHWH's acts.
14: Olam (everlasting, disp 3) is flagged -- who among us can live with the devouring fire? Who among us can dwell with everlasting burnings? The sinners in Zion ask the question that v15-16 will answer. Olam is rendered everlasting -- absolute rather than age-long -- because the consuming fire of divine holiness is being described in its eternal, unchanging quality. The terror of Zion's sinners before the holy God in their midst is the starting point for the righteous-path description that follows.
15: Tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) is flagged -- the one who walks righteously. The answer to v14's question: the person who walks tsedaqot (righteousness-paths) and speaks mesharim (uprightly). The MKT's righteously and honestly cover both the moral and the verbal dimensions. The list of negatives (refusing unjust gain, shaking hands free of bribes, stopping ears against murder-plots, closing eyes against evil) delineates righteousness by what it refuses.
16: Aman (believe/reliable, disp 2) is flagged -- rendered in the phrase his bread will be provided (nitan: given/provided) and his water will be reliable (ne'emanim: faithful/sure). The righteous person dwells on the heights in a fortress of rock -- both images of security beyond the reach of siege and famine. Ne'emanim is the adjective from the aman root: reliable, trustworthy water that does not dry up or fail. The MKT's reliable is a good rendering of this faithfulness-dimension.
17: Melek (king, disp 2) is flagged -- your eyes will see the king in his beauty. The eyes that were closed against evil (v15) will now see the king. The king in his beauty is the eschatological king -- the righteous ruler of Isaiah 9 and 11 revealed in his full glory. The MKT's in his beauty is literal: beyofi means in his loveliness/handsomeness. The land stretching far away extends the vision beyond the siege-compressed walls of Jerusalem to a spacious future.
18: The heart will meditate on the terror -- looking back from the eschatological peace to remember how threatening the Assyrian bureaucracy was. The questions -- where is the scribe, the tribute-tallier, the tower-counter? -- are the questions of relief: all those oppressive mechanisms of empire are gone. The MKT's tallied the tribute is a vivid rendering of the Assyrian fiscal administration that demanded payment from conquered peoples.
19: The fierce people with incomprehensible speech -- the Assyrian administrators whose language (Akkadian) was foreign and whose contemptuous tone was alien. The deep speech and strange tongue describe a language too guttural to understand, used by people who mocked and dominated (scorned) those they controlled. The MKT's too deep to make out is a good rendering of the unintelligibility -- not just different but deliberately opaque.
20: Zion as the city of appointed festivals (moed) -- the three annual pilgrimage feasts of Israel. The festival-city becomes the stable tent: not one of its pegs will be pulled out, not one of its ropes broken. The tent-metaphor is striking for a permanent city -- Jerusalem is imagined as the ideal dwelling, combining the stability of a city with the intimate presence of a tent. The MKT's dwelling at peace captures naveh shanan: a peaceful pasture-home.
21: YHWH is flagged -- the LORD in his glory will be for us a place of broad rivers and streams. The divine name himself is the river -- YHWH replacing the geographical feature. Instead of the Nile giving Egypt its fertility or the Euphrates giving Babylon its wealth, the LORD himself will be the river of life for Jerusalem. No galley or warship can sail these rivers -- they are beyond the reach of military power. The MKT's in his glory preserves the kavod dimension of the LORD's presence as Jerusalem's waterway.
22: YHWH is flagged three times; melek (king, disp 2) is flagged; shaphat (judge, disp 2) is flagged. The triple identification -- the LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king -- is the eschatological fulfillment of Israel's governance structure. All three offices (judicial, legislative, executive) are held by the single divine person, the LORD. The MKT preserves all three divine titles applied to the same subject. He will save us -- the savior-role implicit in all three offices is made explicit.
23: The rigging metaphor -- applied to the Assyrian war-machine described as a ship (or to Jerusalem's enemies) -- suggests a vessel unable to sail: slack ropes, unstable mast, no sail. In the context of v21 (no warship on the LORD's rivers), the slack-rigged enemy ship cannot even float on the divine river. Then the plunder will be so great that even the lame will share in it -- no one will be excluded from the eschatological bounty.
24: No inhabitant of Zion will say I am sick -- the physical healing of the city's people accompanies the spiritual healing: the iniquity of the people who dwell there will be forgiven (nissa avon: lifted/carried, using the Servant's bearing-language of Isaiah 53:4,12). The pair of healing -- bodily sickness removed, guilt lifted -- anticipates Jesus' question in Mark 2:9 (which is easier, to forgive sins or to heal?) by showing that the eschatological state has both as simultaneous gifts.
